                                                                  Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100386


                                                                     Contents lists available at ScienceDirect


                                                             Egyptian Informatics Journal
                                                                journal homepage: www.sciencedirect.com


Full length article

Reliable prediction of software defects using Shapley interpretable machine
learning models
Yazan Al-Smadi a, Mohammed Eshtay b, Ahmad Al-Qerem a, Shadi Nashwan c, *, Osama Ouda c,
A.A. Abd El-Aziz d, e
a
  Department of Computer Science, Faculty of Information Technology, Zarqa University, Zarqa 13110, Jordan
b
  Luminus Technical University College, Amman 11118, Jordan
c
  Computer Science Department, Computer and Information Sciences College, Jouf University, Sakaka 72388, Saudi Arabia
d
  Information System Department, Computer and Information Sciences College, Jouf University, Sakaka 72388, Saudi Arabia
e
  Information Systems & Technology Department, Faculty of Graduate Studies for Statistical Research, Cairo UNI, Giza, Egypt




A R T I C L E I N F O                                      A B S T R A C T

Keywords:                                                  Predicting defect-prone software components can play a significant role in allocating relevant testing resources to
Software Defect Prediction                                 fault-prone modules and hence increasing the business value of software projects. Most of the current software
Feature importance                                         defect prediction studies utilize traditional supervised machine learning algorithms to predict defects in software
Machine learning
                                                           applications. The software datasets utilized in such studies are imbalanced and therefore the reported results
Model interpretation
Shapley Additive Explanation
                                                           cannot be reliably used to judge their performance. Moreover, it is important to explain the output of machine
                                                           learning models employed in fault-predication techniques to determine the contribution of each utilized feature
                                                           to the model output. In this paper, we propose a new framework for predicting software defects utilizing eleven
                                                           machine learning classifiers over twelve different datasets. For feature selection, we employ four different
                                                           nature-inspired search algorithms, namely, particle swarm optimization, genetic algorithm, harmony algorithm,
                                                           and ant colony optimization. Moreover, we make use of the synthetic minority oversampling technique (SMOTE)
                                                           to address the problem of data imbalance. Furthermore, we utilize the Shapley additive explanation model for
                                                           highlighting the highest determinative features. The obtained results demonstrate that gradient boosting, sto­
                                                           chastic gradient boosting, decision trees, and categorical boosting outperform others tested model with over 90%
                                                           accuracy and ROC-AUC. Additionally, we found that the ant colony optimization technique outperforms the
                                                           other tested feature extraction techniques.




1. Introduction                                                                                  software development efforts.
                                                                                                     Software organizations are seeing significant expansion and an in­
    Nowadays, the globe has seen tremendous growth and application of                            crease in the demand for software projects in numerous medical, mili­
technology in a variety of essential industries. As a consequence, soft­                         tary, and industrial domains. However, to our best knowledge, it is
ware quality is the most critical part of software system development.                           difficult to produce faultless software projects. Therefore, the process of
Software testing, on the other hand, is a well-known quality assurance                           detecting and controlling software defects is critical to the project’s
activity that aims to evaluate the internal and external quality aspects of                      success or failure. A software defect, according to the International
a given software module, subsystem, and component using predefined                               Software Testing Qualifications Board (ISTQB) [4], is a problem in a
criteria under certain conditions [1]. The process of ensuring high                              specific software component or system that causes the component or
software quality is inextricably linked to the presence of software flaws.                       system to fail to execute its necessary functions. Most software defects
However, a reliable software system is deemed to be good [2]. For many                           are caused by fundamental development flaws such as insufficient
years, artificial intelligence (AI) technologies [3] were used to face                           coding and design experience, confusing software requirements and
several challenges in software quality aspects, including forecasting                            documentation, insufficient test cases, and poor team communication
software flaws at early stages, software size and cost prediction, and                           [5].

Finally, we employ Shapley additive explanations, a well-designed tool         to clean missing values and balance the data. The datasets, however,
that aids in the explanation of prediction models by highlighting the          were devoid of missing values, noisy data, and missing attributes.
most valuable features of the predictive models [31]. Also, it is a good
visualization tool to show how much a single observation or a set of           3.2. Data balancing
sequential data samples can affect the prediction process by calculating
SHAP values. The SHAP model explanation is shown in Fig. 3.                        Imbalanced data, also known as imbalanced target classes, is a data
                                                                               distribution problem that causes machine learning classifiers to be
3.1. Data collection and analysis                                              biased toward one category over others, negatively affecting prediction
                                                                               [32]. NASA datasets, on the other hand, exhibit a wide distribution gap
    The National Aeronautics and Space Administration dataset is made          across target classes. As a result, this study is based on the employment
up of thirteen datasets that describe various software components. In          of SMOTE for data balance. SMOTE is an oversampling technique that
this study, we employ twelve NASA MDP datasets to predict software             generates synthetic data samples to increase minority class data samples
defects. The KC4 dataset, on the other hand, was omitted owing to              by locating the nearest K - neighbors, computing the distance between
missing attributes and values. The data were obtained from an online           them then multiplying a random value between 0 and 1 for the new
repository, which can be found at https://figshare.com/collections/            sample [33].

3.3. Features selection                                                            3.4. Classification models

    The process of selecting the most informative predictors of existing               In this paper, we propose a new software defect prediction frame­
features by removing highly correlated features is known as feature                work comprised of the following eleven machine learning models: Sta­
selection. In this paper, we apply four bio-inspired search-based feature          tistical classifiers (logistic regression), K-nearest neighbors approach,
selection algorithms.                                                              support vector machine-based classifiers (Support vector machine),
                                                                                   Decision trees approach (J48), and numerous ensemble methods
3.3.1. Particle swarm optimization (PSO)                                           (Stacking, random forest, adaptive boosting, gradient boosting, sto­
    PSO is a nature-inspired optimization algorithm influenced by birds’           chastic gradient boosting, extreme gradient boosting, and categorical
behavior. It’s a population-based algorithm in which each bird is                  boosting).
referred to as a particle and a group of birds is referred to as a swarm (i.
e., population). The core notion of PSO is that each particle represents           3.4.1. Logistic regression
the best solution to the problem. However, following the random                       A logistic regression model, like a linear regression model, predicts a
initialization, the location of each particle is modified, resulting in new        dependent variable by examining the relationship between independent
positions: best previous position PKi and best global positionPKg as shown         data variables. However, it may be utilized for true or false binary
in Fig. 4. Where r1 and r2 represent randomly generated values between             predictions rather than continuous predictions [40]. Furthermore, it fits
[0 and 1]. Also, c1 and c2 represent constant values that update the               an s-shape line using the sigmoid function wherehθ (x) = 1/(1 +
                                                                                          T
weight of Pi (previous position of ith particle) and Pg (previous position         e− [03B8] .x), causing the predicted value to range between 0 and 1, giving
of all particles) [34].                                                            us the possibility that y = 1, which is true based on the × value.
                                                                                   Furthermore, a high × value indicates a high probability that y = 1, but
3.3.2. Genetic algorithm (GA)                                                      an intermediate × value indicates a 50% likelihood that y = 1 when the
    A genetic algorithm is a meta-heuristic that is population-based and           anticipated Y equals 0.5.
is used to address optimization problems. It is a chromosome-inspired
search-based algorithm. GA passes through the following stages:                    3.4.2. K-nearest neighbors
Initialize the population, use the fitness function, selection, and repro­            KNN [41] is a distance-based method that determines the distance
duction [35]. To begin, random individuals are initiated, each of which            between training and test samples. KNN classifier is a sluggish algorithm
has a parameter known as a gene, which creates chromosomes. The core               since it learns during the evaluation phase and saves data samples
idea behind GA is that each individual offers a solution to a problem.             during the learning stage. Without standing, the KNN passes through
However, each individual is evaluated by a fitness score using the fitness         many phases. Begin by determining the value of K (number of nearest
function to determine whether or not the individual is suitable for                samples), then calculate the distance between the training samples and
reproduction. Following the selection process, the crossover function              the new data sample using a distance function. Furthermore, the dis­
can be used to generate a new individual [11]. Fig. 5 shows the GA                 tance values will be sorted and the nearest samples identified based on
process.                                                                           the value of K, and the prediction value will be determined based on the
                                                                                   majority of the class values.
3.3.3. Harmony search algorithm (HA)
    Harmony search is a population-based meta-heuristic algorithm. It              3.4.3. Decision trees
was inspired by the musical practice of finding the best harmonies. The                DT [42] is a tree decision-based classifier that divides the qualities of
entire point of HA is to emulate the musicians’ approach to discover the           the training data into parts. In DT, predictions are created by making
optimal solution. However, HA passes through multiple phases, which                judgments based on a series of questions that are closely relevant to the
are as follows: Initialization of parameters, initialization of harmony            prediction’s aim. It is a non-parametric approach that does not neces­
memory, improvisation of new harmony, and updating harmony mem­                    sitate the use of a mapping function between predictors and target data.
ory [36]. Following the initialization of the basic parameters: harmony            The process of picking the optimum decision values at each depth level
memory (HM) size, harmony memory consideration rate (HMCR), and                    is referred to as impurity in DT. The Gini-index function, on the other
pitch adjustment rate (PAR). The harmony memory matrix is designed                 hand, may be used to measure the impurity of the tree.
and filled at random with the values of the possible variables. Each row
in the matrix indicates a potential solution. However, relying on HMCR             3.4.4. Random forests
and PAR, additional vectors are generated. Finally, if the new harmony                 RF [43] is a bagging ensemble-based classifier. As the basic learners,
vectors outperform the old ones, they will be preserved as the best so­            it divides the training data into several decision trees. RF is also a non-
lution in the HM [37]. The HA pseudocode is shown in Fig. 6.                       parametric model. However, a restricted random number of rows is
                                                                                   chosen from the data population, and a set of decision trees is built for
3.3.4. Ant colony optimization (ACO)                                               each segment to generate classification output. An individual decision
   In several disciplines, ACO is one of the most widely utilized meta-            tree might be bigger than others. A majority vote in RF indicates a meta-
heuristic search-based algorithms. It is an improved version of Marco              classifier, which is a classification decision made based on the judgments
Dorigo’s original algorithm, the ant system, which was introduced in               of the basic learners. It supports numeric and categorical data types, as
1992 [38]. ACO was inspired by the foraging activity of ant colonies.              well as complicated predictor functions.
The goal of ACO is to locate the shortest path between the ant nest and
the supplies. Each route shows a possible solution. However, after the             3.4.5. Support vector machine
pathways have been initialized, and with the assistance of ant phero­                  A support vector machine [44] is a classifier that uses hyperplanes to
mones, which are organic chemical substances, ants travel the shortest             find the best decision boundary in dimensional space based on the
path to the nest, which represents the ideal solution. Many more models            number of features. However, because the hyperplane has numerous
have been used and enhanced with ACO. To solve the challenge of                    options, the goal of the support vector machine is to pick the ideal hy­
autonomous surface vehicles, Dimitrios [39] suggested an updated                   perplane with the largest margins among data samples to improve
version of ACO based on fuzzy logic. The ACO pseudocode is shown in                classification accuracy. Additionally, linear and non-linear classification
Fig. 7.                                                                            can be done. Generally, a support vector machine performs better and is
                                                                                   less prone to overfitting when the class distribution is known. However,


                                                                               6
Y. Al-Smadi et al.                                                                                                     Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100386




                                                 Fig. 4. Particle Swarm Optimization Architecture Illustration.




                                                            Fig. 5. Genetic Algorithm Pseudocode.


when maximum margins are employed, new data may be appropriately                    AdaBoost pseudocode.
fit and categorized.
                                                                                    3.4.7. Gradient boosting
3.4.6. Adaptive boosting                                                                GB [46] is a boosting ensemble-based algorithm for classification and
    AdaBoost [45] is an ensemble-based boosting classifier. In contrast to          regression that employs the idea of pseudo residuals (PR). Unlike Ada­
decision trees and random forests. Several decision trees are merged                Boost, gradient boosting begins with a single leaf that reflects the
independently in AdaBoost. Each of these contains a single node and two             average values of anticipated classes rather than a stump. GB, on the
leaves, forming what is known as a stump. A stumps forest is a collection           other hand, creates a fixed-sized tree, similar to AdaBoost, where each
of stumps. However, stumps are poor classification learners. AdaBoost’s             tree can be larger than a stump. Decision trees are used as base learners
entire concept is to combine numerous weak learners for classification.             in GB. The difference between the actual and anticipated values is rep­
The sequence of stump creation is critical in AdaBoost since each pro­              resented by PR, which may be determined by fitting a linear regression
duced tree influences the output of the following one. Each data sample             line as a loss function. In GB, prediction errors may be reduced by
is weighted (w) to determine the order of stumps where w = 1/Total                  frequently updating PR values from tree to tree, making it a powerful
number of samples. However, sample weight is updated frequently by                  prediction approach. Fig. 9 shows the GB pseudocode.
sw = wold *eamountofsay(α) . Also, the Gini-Index may be used to rank stumps,
with the lower the Gini value, the more essential the stump. Fig. 8 shows

                                                                                7
Y. Al-Smadi et al.                                                  Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100386




                     Fig. 6. Harmony Search Algorithm Pseudocode.




                      Fig. 7. Ant Colony Optimization pseudocode.




                         Fig. 8. Adaptive Boosting pseudocode.




                                           8
Y. Al-Smadi et al.                                                                                                      Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100386




                                                               Fig. 9. Gradient Boosting Pseudocode.


                                                                                     3.4.8. Stochastic gradient boosting
                                                                                         Friedman [47] introduced the stochastic gradient boosting approach,
                                                                                     which is an ensemble-based method. The hybrid procedure of homog­
                                                                                     enous (boosting and bagging) approaches inspired it. By weighting the
                                                                                     base learners, it may be utilized for regression and classification. Deci­
                                                                                     sion trees, on the other hand, are the most widely used SGB base
                                                                                     learners. Each tree, on the other hand, is trained by selecting a random
                                                                                     number of data sample records. SGB, as compared to bagging methods,
                                                                                     is an effective instrument for limiting the chance of overfitting by
                                                                                     randomly eliminating a portion of input data [48]. SGB has been utilized
                                                                                     in a variety of areas for many years. Sérgio Godinho et al., [49]
                                                                                     employed SGB for multi-image classification in their inquiry study.

                                                                                     3.4.9. Extreme gradient boosting
                                                                                         XGBoost [50] is an ensemble method based on boosting. It is a more
                                                                                     advanced version of gradient boosting. It was also created for large and
                     Fig. 10. ROC-AUC ranges and values.                             intricate datasets. Unlike adaptive and gradient boosting, the XGBoost
                                                                                     employs unique regression trees. Forming what is known as born-again
                                                                                     trees. XGBoost tree formation, on the other hand, begins with a single
Table 3                                                                              leaf, much like gradient boosting. Furthermore, gradient boosting and
Boosting Hyperparameters Grid-Search Space.                                          regularization are critical steps in XGBoost. For many years, XGBoost
  Parameters                               Grid-Search Space                         has been one of the most effective boosting methods. In their experi­
  Number of estimators                     50, 70, 90, 100, 150, 120, 180, 200       mental investigation, Ismail and Faisal [51] demonstrated that XGBoost
  Learning rate                            0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10                   outperforms other classifiers such as RF, SVM, radial basis function
  Loss function                            Deviance, exponential                     neural network, and naive Bayes. It is simple to use and provides a high
  Algorithms                               SAMME, SAMME.R                            level of discriminative prediction accuracy. In addition to its ability to

handle large data distribution with imbalanced target classes.                         3.4.11. Ensemble stacking
                                                                                           Stacking [54] is a heterogeneous ensemble-based method. Stacking,
3.4.10. Categorical boosting                                                           as opposed to bagging and boosting, divides a machine learning model
    CatBoost is a more advanced kind of gradient boosting. It was pro­                 into two layers. Stacking proceeds in numerous stages: Dataset gathering
posed in 2018 by Anna Veronika Dorogush and the Yandex corporate                       and features extractor, base learner construction, and Meta learner
team [52]. It was created to be the optimum option for heterogeneous                   creation. The base layer has N distinct classifiers. To begin, training data
data, as opposed to bagging and stacking. Based on a boosting approach,                is divided into K portions. Each base learner produces a prediction based
it was designed to handle categorical and numerical features. CatBoost is              on K. A Meta dataset, on the other hand, integrates the prediction out­
an open-source package that allows for extremely rapid graphics pro­                   comes of base learners. A Meta classifier is the second level of prediction.
cessing unit (GPU) and central processing unit (CPU) calculations. Very                It generates prediction output based on previous predictions. In this
effective for small datasets and simple to utilize. It is also a decision tree-        work, we employ the use of RF and SVM as base learners. Also, we use
based method, which has the benefit of decreasing overfitting. CatBoost                LG as a Meta classifier.
has long been a rival to other gradient boosting methods. Jengei Hong
[53] found that CatBoost outperformed XGBoost and LightGBM imple­                      4. Evaluation metrics and hyperparameters tuning
mentations in his experimental investigation on predicting house
pricing.                                                                                   Several evaluation metrics may be used to evaluate machine learning
                                                                                       classifiers, including accuracy, precision, recall, receiver operating
                                                                                       characteristics (ROC), F-measure, and area under the curve (AUC). A

confusion matrix, on the other hand, is a table that contains a collection             and false positive rate (1-specificity) are two threshold values that have
of characteristics that may be used to describe the performance of a                   an inverse relationship, with sensitivity increasing as specificity de­
classifier [55]. It may be used to visualize and compare the statistical               creases and vice versa. The higher the AUC, however, the better, indi­
performance of various algorithms.                                                     cating that the classifier is not doing random classification. Fig. 10
    In short, a confusion matrix has four key properties that may be                   shows the AUC ranges and values. 20% of the data were maintained as
utilized to derive different evaluation metrics, which are as follows:                 testing sets to evaluate classification accuracy. The confusion matrix
                                                                                       may be used to extract all of the statistic measures listed above as
 • True positive (TP): Presents the number of defective modules that                   follows:
   have been classified correctly as defective modules.
 • True negative (TN): Presents the number of non-defective modules                                     TP+TN
                                                                                        • Accuracy = TP+FP+TN+FN
   that have been classified correctly as non-defective modules.                                                            TP
                                                                                        • Turepositiverate(Sensitivity) = TP+FN
 • False positive (FP): Presents the number of misclassified non-
                                                                                                                                      FP
   defective modules that are defective.                                                • Falsepositiverate = 1 − specificity = 1 − TN+FP
 • False negative (FN): Presents the number of misclassified defective
   modules that are non-defective modules.                                             In this work, we follow the use of the Grid-Search method for tuning and
                                                                                       optimizing classifiers’ hyperparameters. Grid-Search, on the other hand,
    In this study, we evaluate the proposed classifiers using testing ac­              is a search strategy for choosing the optimum parameters by merging all
curacy and AUC. AUC, on the other hand, is a performance metric that                   of the possible value entries in the search space [57]. Boosting ensemble
indicates how well a certain classifier performs classification by dis­                methods parameters including the number of estimators, learning rate,
tinguishing between target classes [56]. True positive rate (sensitivity)              loss function, and algorithms were tuned based on a set of values. Also, 5

  LR                               0.925000                 0.862816                    0.862816                  0.862816                       0.860262

  Decision trees                   0.964286                 0.924188                    0.924188                  0.924188                       0.978166
  Random Forest                    0.928571                 0.935018                    0.931408                  0.938628                       0.958515
  Ada Boost                        0.928571                 0.935018                    0.931408                  0.938628                       0.958515
  Gradient Boosting                0.975000                 0.938628                    0.935018                  0.942238                       0.973799
  SGB                              0.971429                 0.942238                    0.938628                  0.945848                       0.931507
  XGBoost                          0.860714                 0.469314                    0.758123                  0.866426                       0.871179
  Stacking                         0.921429                 0.927536                    0.927536                  0.927536                       0.890830
  Cat Boost                        0.971429                 0.942238                    0.942238                  0.942238                       0.982533
  SVM                              0.871429                 0.942238                    0.942238                  0.942238                       0.901747
  Accuracy (AVG)                   0.926                    0.89                        0.917                     0.931                          0.938

ranging from 45 to 57 percent. To be clear, the reason is most likely           tion, at 25.17, relative to the base value of 0.6, however, the design

hyperparameters. To the best of our understanding, boosting methods in          lines of code comments, design density, and Halsted content metrics
